content.text: A Service Level Objective (SLO) is the target value for an SLI over a defined time window. If the SLI measures 'fraction of requests returning 2xx', the SLO states the target: 'at least 99.9% of requests must return 2xx over any rolling 30-day window'. The SLO is an internal commitment the engineering team makes to themselves and to stakeholders — it is not a customer contract (that is the SLA).

SLOs serve two functions: they define when a service is 'good enough' to release changes, and they define when a service is 'failing' and action is required. Without a defined SLO, both decisions become subjective, inconsistent, and political.
An SLO without an error budget is just a number. The error budget is what turns the SLO into an operational policy.

The error budget is the amount of unreliability your SLO allows. It is derived directly from the SLO target and the measurement window. If your SLO is 99.9% availability over a 30-day window, your error budget is the 0.1% of requests that may fail — which translates to approximately 43.2 minutes of complete unavailability per 30 days, or a larger number of partial failures spread across the window.

attrs.alt: Error budget consumption over 30-day window showing P0 and P1 incident impacts

content.text: These figures assume complete unavailability — every request fails. In practice, partial degradation (10% of requests failing) consumes the budget at 10% the rate of full unavailability, so 43.2 minutes of full downtime equals 432 minutes of 10% degradation at the same SLO cost.

An error budget is only useful if it has an associated policy: what happens when the budget is at 50%? At 25%? At 0%? The policy must be agreed upon before the budget is consumed, not after — post-hoc negotiation under pressure produces inconsistent decisions.
Normal operations. Deploy freely. Run experiments.
Elevated caution. No experiments. Risky deploys require SRE review.
Reliability freeze. No feature deploys. Only bug fixes and reliability work.
Incident posture. All hands on reliability. No feature work until budget recovers.

There are two common failure modes in SLO target setting: targets set aspirationally high (99.999%) that are never met and create constant alert fatigue, and targets set defensively low (99%) that permit so much unreliability that users notice. The correct approach is to set targets based on data and user tolerance, not on how impressive the number looks.

Before setting an SLO, measure the service's actual historical availability and latency over the last 90 days. The SLO target should be achievable given current infrastructure — starting from observed reality is more credible than starting from an aspiration.
The right SLO is one where breaching it causes detectable user harm — but not so tight that normal engineering activity consumes the budget. User tolerance varies by use case:
Very low — users expect instant response

Set the SLO target slightly below the observed historical performance. If your service has been achieving 99.95% availability historically, do not set the SLO at 99.95% — set it at 99.9%. The 0.05% gap is your safety margin: it allows you to make changes without immediately burning your error budget every time you touch the service.
SLO = observed_availability - safety_margin. A margin of 0.1–0.5 percentage points is typical. If you cannot afford any safety margin, your service is already at the edge of its reliability.
Step 4: Agree with Stakeholders Before Launch

SLOs must be agreed upon with product owners and customer-facing stakeholders before the service goes live. An SLO agreed to after launch is hard to lower (it looks like lowering quality standards) and hard to raise without investment (it requires reliability work). Set it once with full context.

Waiting until the error budget is exhausted before alerting means the damage is already done. Burn rate alerting fires when the budget is being consumed faster than it can sustain over the measurement window — giving you time to intervene before exhaustion.

A burn rate of 1 means the budget is being consumed exactly at the rate that would exhaust it in the measurement window. A burn rate of 14 means the budget is being consumed 14 times faster than sustainable — at that rate, a 30-day budget is exhausted in about 50 hours.

# burn_rate.py — compute SLO burn rate from error rate
def burn_rate(
    error_rate: float,
    slo_target: float,
) -> float:
    """
    error_rate: fraction of bad requests in the window (e.g. 0.02 = 2% error rate)
    slo_target: SLO as a fraction (e.g. 0.999 = 99.9% availability SLO)

    Returns: burn rate (1 = sustainable, >1 = burning faster than sustainable)

    Formula: burn_rate = error_rate / (1 - slo_target)
    At burn_rate = 1: budget is consumed at exactly the rate that exhausts it in the window.
    At burn_rate = 14: budget is exhausted 14x faster → 30-day budget gone in ~2 days.
    """
    budget_fraction = 1.0 - slo_target
    if budget_fraction == 0:
        return float('inf')
    return error_rate / budget_fraction


# Example: 99.9% SLO, current error rate 1.4%
rate = burn_rate(error_rate=0.014, slo_target=0.999)
print(f"Burn rate: {rate:.1f}x")  # → 14.0x
# Interpretation: the 30-day budget will be exhausted in ~2 days if this rate continues.

The Google SRE workbook recommends a two-window burn rate alert: a fast-burn detection window (short window, high burn rate threshold) and a slow-burn detection window (long window, lower burn rate threshold). Using two windows catches both sudden outages and prolonged degradations.
Acute outage consuming ≥50% of 30-day budget in 1h
Sustained moderate degradation before it becomes critical

# Prometheus alerting rules — multi-window burn rate for 99.9% SLO
groups:
  - name: slo.rules
    rules:
      # P0: fast burn — 14x for 5 min AND 1h
      - alert: ClaudeIntegrationFastBurnP0
        expr: |
          (
            sum(rate(http_requests_total{service="claude-integration",status_code=~"5.."}[5m]))
            /
            sum(rate(http_requests_total{service="claude-integration",status_code!~"4.."}[5m]))
          ) / (1 - 0.999) >= 14
          and
          (
            sum(rate(http_requests_total{service="claude-integration",status_code=~"5.."}[1h]))
            /
            sum(rate(http_requests_total{service="claude-integration",status_code!~"4.."}[1h]))
          ) / (1 - 0.999) >= 14
        for: 2m
        labels:
          severity: page
          slo: claude-integration-availability
        annotations:
          summary: "SLO fast burn: 14x rate, 50% of 30-day budget consumed in 1h if sustained"

      # P1: slow burn — 3x for 6h AND 24h
      - alert: ClaudeIntegrationSlowBurnP1
        expr: |
          (
            sum(rate(http_requests_total{service="claude-integration",status_code=~"5.."}[6h]))
            /
            sum(rate(http_requests_total{service="claude-integration",status_code!~"4.."}[6h]))
          ) / (1 - 0.999) >= 3
          and
          (
            sum(rate(http_requests_total{service="claude-integration",status_code=~"5.."}[24h]))
            /
            sum(rate(http_requests_total{service="claude-integration",status_code!~"4.."}[24h]))
          ) / (1 - 0.999) >= 3
        for: 5m
        labels:
          severity: ticket
          slo: claude-integration-availability
        annotations:
          summary: "SLO slow burn: 3x rate over 24h, budget depleting steadily"

The error budget policy's most practical application is governing deploys. When the budget is healthy (>50%), deploys should proceed without extra friction. When the budget is low, deploys carry risk — every incident that a deploy causes will consume what little budget remains. The linkage is mechanical: the deployment pipeline checks the current error budget state and gates accordingly.

# deployment_gate.py — check error budget before allowing a deploy
import requests
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class ErrorBudgetStatus:
    slo_target: float
    window_days: int
    allowed_error_fraction: float   # 1 - slo_target
    consumed_error_fraction: float  # actual error fraction in window
    budget_remaining_fraction: float  # 1 - (consumed / allowed)

    @property
    def budget_pct(self) -> float:
        return self.budget_remaining_fraction * 100

    @property
    def deploy_allowed(self) -> bool:
        return self.budget_pct > 25.0  # 25% = freeze threshold

    @property
    def policy_level(self) -> str:
        pct = self.budget_pct
        if pct > 50: return "normal"
        if pct > 25: return "elevated_caution"
        if pct > 0:  return "reliability_freeze"
        return "incident_posture"


def get_error_budget_status(
    prometheus_url: str,
    service: str,
    slo_target: float = 0.999,
    window_days: int = 30,
) -> ErrorBudgetStatus:
    window = f"{window_days}d"
    good_query = (
        f'sum(rate(http_requests_total{{service="{service}",status_code=~"2.."}}[{window}]))'
    )
    total_query = (
        f'sum(rate(http_requests_total{{service="{service}",status_code!~"4.."}}[{window}]))'
    )
    good = float(requests.get(f"{prometheus_url}/api/v1/query",
        params={"query": good_query}).json()["data"]["result"][0]["value"][1])
    total = float(requests.get(f"{prometheus_url}/api/v1/query",
        params={"query": total_query}).json()["data"]["result"][0]["value"][1])

    actual_availability = good / total if total > 0 else 1.0
    actual_error_fraction = 1.0 - actual_availability
    allowed_error_fraction = 1.0 - slo_target
    budget_remaining = 1.0 - (actual_error_fraction / allowed_error_fraction)

    return ErrorBudgetStatus(
        slo_target=slo_target,
        window_days=window_days,
        allowed_error_fraction=allowed_error_fraction,
        consumed_error_fraction=actual_error_fraction,
        budget_remaining_fraction=max(0.0, budget_remaining),
    )


# Usage in CI/CD pipeline
status = get_error_budget_status("http://prometheus:9090", "claude-integration")
print(f"Budget remaining: {status.budget_pct:.1f}% — policy: {status.policy_level}")

if not status.deploy_allowed:
    raise SystemExit(
        f"Deploy blocked: error budget at {status.budget_pct:.1f}% "
        f"(below 25% freeze threshold). Reliability work only until budget recovers."
    )
The measurement window is how long a single error budget cycle lasts. The two most common choices are 30-day rolling and calendar month. They have different operational properties.
Budget updates continuously as old events age out
Budget resets hard on the 1st of each month
No cliff; past events smoothly drop out
Budget pressure maximized at month-end; resets fully on 1st
Slow — incidents age out gradually over 30 days
Fast — a bad incident in week 1 vanishes on the 1st
Higher — teams understand monthly budgets intuitively
Services with high traffic and frequent incidents
Services with low traffic and infrequent incidents

For Claude API integration services, use a 30-day rolling window. The continuous update prevents 'budget cliffs' at month-end and ensures the error budget accurately reflects the service's recent behavior.

Most Claude API integration services have multiple SLOs — availability, latency, and completeness at minimum. Each SLO has its own error budget and its own consumption rate. The question of which budget governs deployment decisions is a policy choice: most teams use the most-depleted budget (the lowest remaining percentage across all SLOs) as the deployment gate.
Setting 99.99% for a service that historically achieves 99.5%: the budget is exhausted immediately and no work can proceed.

Setting different SLO windows for different SLIs on the same service: availability on a 30-day window and latency on a 7-day window makes deployment policy incoherent. Use the same window for all SLOs on a service.

Using the SLA (customer contract) number as the SLO: the SLA is a floor, not a target. Your SLO should be tighter than your SLA — so a breach of the SLO triggers internal action before the SLA is actually violated.
Not updating SLOs when the service changes substantially: a new model tier, a new request type, or a new deployment region may require recalibrating SLO targets.
Running the error budget policy only when convenient: the policy only works if it governs every deploy, not just the ones the on-call team happens to review manually.

An SLO target is the minimum acceptable value for an SLI over a defined window. The error budget — derived as (1 - SLO target) × total requests — quantifies how much unreliability is permitted. The error budget policy converts that number into operational decisions: at 50% remaining, caution is elevated; at 25%, deploys freeze; at 0%, all hands pivot to reliability. Burn rate alerting fires before exhaustion by detecting when the budget is being consumed faster than sustainable — multi-window alerts (5 min + 1h for P0, 6h + 24h for P1) catch both acute outages and prolonged degradations. SLO targets should be set based on historical performance minus a safety margin, agreed with stakeholders before launch, and never set tighter than the service can realistically sustain.
Error budget = (1 - SLO target) × measurement window. 99.9% SLO = 43.2 minutes of full downtime per 30 days.
Error budget policy must be written before launch: what deploys are allowed at 50%, 25%, and 0% remaining.
Burn rate = error_rate / (1 - SLO_target). Rate of 14x means the budget is exhausted in 1/14th of the window.
Multi-window burn rate alerts (P0: 14x on 5min+1h, P1: 3x on 6h+24h) catch both acute and sustained degradation.
SLO targets: start from 90-day historical performance, subtract a 0.1–0.5% safety margin, agree with stakeholders.
Use the same measurement window (30-day rolling) for all SLOs on a service to keep deployment policy coherent.
